topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The bouquet sat on the chipped coffee table, a vibrant, almost offensively cheerful arrangement of lilies and roses. I stared at it, the petals feeling alien and too bright under the harsh overhead light. No note. Just flowers, dropped into my living room like some unsolicited offering. I picked up a stem, the silk cool against my fingertips, and let it drop. It landed with a soft, pathetic sigh onto the worn rug. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck knotting painfully. Why? What did this gesture signify? A misplaced attempt at reconciliation? I pushed myself up from the sofa, the movement stiff, and walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape. The air in the room suddenly felt too thin, too charged with unspoken disappointment.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed over the wrapping, my hand barely registered the transaction. It was just a bundle of blooms, wrapped in cheap cellophane, devoid of any accompanying sentiment. I took the package with a practiced economy of motion, my gaze fixed on the street below, refusing to meet the eyes of the person who had just presented it. Inside, the scent was cloying—too sweet, too saccharine. I carried it to the kitchen, setting it down on the counter with a deliberate, heavy placement. I didn't bother to examine the arrangement; I just turned away, focusing instead on the rhythmic clinking of ice in the distant refrigerator. Every nerve ending felt frayed, reacting to the sheer, unearned display of affection.

[story 3]

I found the flowers tucked behind the mail, a stark splash of color against the monochrome pile of bills and junk flyers. My fingers brushed against the velvety texture of a deep purple orchid. A sharp, involuntary shudder ran through me, and I quickly pulled my hand back, as if burned. I didn't move to discard them; instead, I held them tightly, pressing the cool stems against my palm until my knuckles turned white. The silence in my apartment amplified the sound of my own shallow breathing. I looked at the arrangement, then at the empty space beside me, a deep, internal resentment rising like bile in my throat. It was an imposition, an unnecessary flourish, and I felt a profound sense of being overlooked, of being utterly irrelevant.
